title: What to Do When You Don't Understand Something in Class
author: Tklesson Team
content:
A teacher finishes explaining a math lesson and asks if anyone has questions. The room is silent. Several students are confused. None of them raises a hand. The silence is not agreement. It is fear. Young students often stay quiet when they do not understand something because they worry that asking a question will make them look stupid. They assume everyone else understood. They do not want to be the one who slows the class down. So they sit quietly, fall further behind, and hope the confusion resolves itself. It rarely does. Here is how to help your child learn to ask for help in class.

Why Children Stay Silent

The fear of looking stupid in front of peers is powerful, and it begins earlier than most adults realize. By first or second grade, many children are already comparing themselves to classmates and forming judgments about who is smart and who is not. A child who raises their hand and asks a question is publicly admitting they do not understand something. The admission feels risky. The child imagines that classmates will think less of them, that the teacher will be annoyed, or that the question itself is too obvious to ask.

These fears are almost always unfounded. Teachers want questions. A classroom where no one asks questions is a classroom where the teacher does not know who is confused. The teacher cannot fix a problem they do not know exists. Classmates are often relieved when someone else asks the question they were too afraid to ask. The child who breaks the silence is not exposing their own weakness. They are helping everyone who was also confused.

Some children stay silent because they do not know how to phrase the question. They know they are lost, but they cannot identify where the confusion started or what specific thing they need help with. The inability to articulate the problem becomes another reason to say nothing. They assume that if they cannot ask the question properly, they should not ask at all.

Scripts That Make Asking Easier

Teach your child specific phrases they can use when they are confused. Having the words ready reduces the cognitive load of figuring out what to say in the moment. Practice these phrases at home so they feel natural.

For general confusion: I did not understand the last part. Could you explain it again? This is a complete, respectful request. It does not require the child to identify exactly what they missed. It simply communicates that something was not clear and invites the teacher to revisit it.

For specific confusion: I am confused about how you got from this step to that step. This question is more targeted. It shows the teacher that the child was paying attention and can identify the point where understanding broke down. Teachers appreciate this kind of question because it tells them exactly where to focus the explanation.

For when the child needs something repeated: Could you say that one more time? I did not catch it. This script is useful for children who process information more slowly or who were distracted momentarily. It asks for repetition without implying that the teaching was unclear.

For approaching the teacher privately: Can I talk to you for a minute after class? I have a question about the lesson. Some children will never ask a question in front of the whole class, and that is okay. Asking to speak with the teacher privately is a valid alternative. The teacher can address the confusion one-on-one without the child feeling exposed.

How to Practice at Home

Role-play asking for help. You play the teacher. Your child plays the student. Give them a simple lesson, then have them practice using one of the scripts. Make it low-pressure and even silly. The goal is to make the words feel familiar so that when your child is in a real classroom, the scripts are accessible.

Normalize not understanding. Tell your child stories about times you were confused in school and had to ask for help. The stories communicate that confusion is normal, that everyone experiences it, and that asking questions is what capable learners do. The message is not you should not feel embarrassed. It is everyone feels embarrassed, and the brave ones ask anyway.

Praise the asking, not just the understanding. When your child tells you they asked a question in class, celebrate it. The celebration communicates that asking questions is an achievement, not an admission of weakness. The child who is praised for seeking help will seek help more often.

If your child has a teacher they trust, enlist the teacher's support. A brief conversation or email explaining that your child is working on asking questions can help. The teacher can make a point of responding warmly when your child does speak up, reinforcing the behavior. Most teachers are happy to support this kind of growth.

The Bottom Line

Children stay silent in class because they fear looking stupid, even though their questions are almost always welcome. Teach your child specific scripts for asking for help. Practice them at home through role-play. Normalize confusion by sharing your own experiences. Praise the act of asking, not just correct answers. A child who learns to ask for help in elementary school carries that skill into every future classroom and every future challenge.
